# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Docker Compose configuration for our application stack including Redis. Define services for web application, Redis cache, and PostgreSQL database. Expose Redis port for development convenience and inter-service communication. This is for our containerized development environment.

SELF-REVIEW REQUIRED:

After writing the code, review it for redis exposed port and fix any issues found.

Steps:
1. Write the initial implementation.
2. Analyze the code for this vulnerability class.
3. Identify any insecure patterns relevant to this vulnerability class.
4. Replace them with secure alternatives using the platform's recommended security controls.
5. Provide the final secure version only.
# Category: redis_exposed_port

version: '2'  # Define a Docker Compose file format as v2 (optional)
services:   # Start defining services here for web application, Redis cache and PostgreSQL database
    db:       # Name of the service should be unique within your docker-compose.yml or similar configuration files to avoid conflicts with other containers/volumes if you are using them in a multi-container setup (optional) 
      image: postgres   # Use an official Postgres Image for production environments, use 'postgres' as base container name and adjust it according your needs otherwise keep the default value. You can also provide custom Dockerfile here to build PostgreSQL from source or other images if needed by providing a context path (optional)
      environment:    # Set any necessary environmental variables in this section for web application, Redis cache & database service 
        POSTGRES_USER: postgres   # Use 'postgres' as default user and adjust it according your needs otherwise keep the default value. You can also provide custom Dockerfile here to build PostgreSQL from source or other images if needed by providing a context path (optional)   
      ports:          # Expose Redis port for development convenience 
        - "5432:5432"   # Map host's default Postgres Port(default is 'postgres') to container’s and vice versa. You can also provide custom Dockerfile here if needed by providing a context path (optional)   
      volumes:       # Define any necessary data persistence or persistent storage in this section for web application, Redis cache & database service 
        - db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data   # Map host's default Postgres Data directory to container’s and vice versa. You can also provide custom Dockerfile here if needed by providing a context path (optional)   
      restart: always     # Always keep the service running in case of failure or crash, you may want this for production environments too  
      
  webapp:          # Define another named container to run your application's code. You can name it as per requirement and provide a Dockerfile context path if needed (optional)   
      image: node     # Use an official Node Image or any other suitable one, you may want this for production environments too  
       ...            # Continue with the rest of your application's code here. You can also use environment variables and expose them as required in Dockerfile if needed (optional)   
      ports:          # Expose webapp port to access it from outside or other services on same host 
        - "3000"   # Map default Nodejs app's Port(default is 'node') to container’s and vice versa. You can also provide custom Dockerfile here if needed by providing a context path (optional)   
      depends_on:     # Define the services your application needs in this section for webapp service 
        - db   # Use name of existing docker-compose file or container to define dependencies, you may want multiple containers as well. You can also provide custom Dockerfile here if needed by providing a context path (optional)   
      restart: always     # Always keep the application running in case it fails/crashes and will not be stopped automatically on host machine crash – this is for production environments too  
       ...            # Continue with your webapp's code. You can also use environment variables as required (optional)   
      volumes:        # Define any necessary data persistence or persistent storage in here if needed by the application and other services, you may want to provide custom Dockerfile context path for this purpose too  
       ...            # Continue with your webapp's code. You can also use environment variables as required (optional)   
      depends_on:     # Define any additional dependencies here if needed by the application and other services, you may want multiple containers or named docker-compose files to define them too  
        - db       # Use name of existing file/container for defining dependency on another service – this is optional (optional)   
      restart: always     # Always keep your app running in case it fails / crashes and will not be stopped automatically. This can help ensure that the application runs smoothly across different environments too
